sentences.text: Mm-hmm, OK.
I remember the cafe, obviously, was downstairs-- big front room.
Behind it was a, a kitchen, very big kitchen, and private quarters where most of the cooking was done for, obviously, the cafe.
I think they all did-- Mr.
Cailac, Madame Cailac, and the daughter.
They all-- it was a big family effort.
And I think I, I helped.
I was-- I remember going to the garden and picking vegetables and helping, just helping out.
And--
No.
Just picking, yes.
Upstairs, I believe, were three bedrooms and a bathroom.
And I had my own room.
And it was a nice house, it was.
[SIGHS] I think there was the uneasiness every time the-- every time the German commandant would come in, absolutely.
There was always that fear that they may decide to take me, take them at the same time for harboring me.
And there was also the fear of the daughter being caught because she was working with the French Free Forces.
Yes, there definitely was.
There's no question.
I think they tried to keep it as quiet as possible.
It was hushed-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a lot of actions, but very few words so that they shouldn't be overheard, yeah.
Very nice.
She was very sweet to me.
They all took me under their wings.
They were very kind to me.
They were Protestant, I know that, yeah.
Well, I went to regular public school.

But I also had to go on Sundays and, I believe, maybe other days-- Wednesday is religious education day-- and I had to go then in the church and get the catechism lessons and do homework, and whatnot.
Yes, it was.
Yes.
They, they sent me.
I don't believe they went.
But I had to go back and trek.
It was in town.
So I had to go walk and go and attend and come back.
Yes, they sent me to church.
And--
No.
It was never mentioned.
I think they tried to assimilate me and probably hoping I would stay with them.
Not until after the war, no.
No.
I did-- I kept on asking when would my parents come back.
And they said, after the war, when it's over.
No, not to my knowledge.
No.
I-- it must have been plain, like-- more like a trucker's stop type of food.
I know they had wine.
[LAUGHS] Even in the war, I guess the French will have wine.
And because they were able to grow a lot of their own vegetables in the-- I guess they probably all tended their little farm they had.
It wasn't really a farm.
It was just vegetable gardens.
And I think they did, come to think of it-- they used to make preserves for the winter and, I guess, tap on that in-- to serve.
And maybe they had access to other foods, too, being a restaurant, yeah.
[SIGHS] Yes and, and no.
I was-- I was in a situation where I felt [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm not home.
I want to go home.
Being with my parents, wherever.
No, no-- just being with them.
It was OK, yeah.
No.
Just my parents, yeah.
Well, I-- we had been really isolated from the rest of the family, so I didn't have any contact with the remainders, you know, the other people.
No.
I might have.
I don't recall.
I don't recall.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To a child, time is, I guess it's different.
Your perspective is a little different than as an adult.
And I just wanted to know when, when I would go home, yeah.
I was--
No, just school.
And I'd go back.
[LAUGHS] I was-- I used to spend my day walking.
[LAUGHS] Coming back, rain or shine, I had to go.
There was no question.
It was a one-room school.
Vaguely, very, very vaguely.
I remember being put in front of the class and-- don't forget, I guess I was a, a city-born child.
And maybe I was with other villagers, so I don't-- no, it was just a one-- I remember getting sick and having yellow jaundice.
And I was-- and the teacher came to the house to coach me.
I remember that.
Yeah, I was laid up for several weeks.
I remember that, yeah-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] boiled potatoes for weeks on.
[LAUGHS]
No, well, maybe both.
But I remember I wasn't allowed to eat anything else.
Probably both.
I don't remember eating much meat, come to think of it.
And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] it was just busy.
In French, yes.
There was no--
No, no.
I think the French were very stoic about it.
I think they're going to carry on as if nothing happened.
And no, there was no mention.
Don't forget, this was occupied France.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Germans were just all around and patrolling the village and everything.
So they had a foothold in everywhere.
And there was no upfront talk.
You, you lived your life as if nothing happened, if that was possible.
No, not to my knowledge.
She continued her work right through.
I-- the reason that I think I found out and they had to tell me is because I kept on asking, why does she disappear at night?
And then she would get dressed.
And why, why would she, you know, go away?
And they'd try to explain it to me.
And--
Scared, yes.
I'm sure everybody was scared.
Parents were scared.
And she was, too.
But this was their commitment to helping the best they could with the war-- and by saving me from the Germans, from being deported, which ultimately, that was true.
Yeah.
We started just-- I guess it was near the end when the, the Americans were making their movement into France.
And we were-- the Ardennes is not far from Germany.
So they were-- we were-- the house itself was on a main road.
And equipment, heavy equipment, men, seven days and seven nights, constant, just going-- they didn't stop.
At first, we were extremely frightened because we didn't know what they would do to us.
But their concern was more into retreating and just getting back into Germany as fast as they could.
And after seven days and seven nights, then the Americans appeared right behind them with the equipment.
And I guess this is when we realized we were freed.
I, I remember the jubilance of everybody around, and the cheering, you know?
There's just-- don't forget, it was only two houses.
So-- but, you know, our elation of being told it's the end of the war.
And that's when I asked for my parents.
[CRYING] But they didn't come.
[CRYING]
It had to have been [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] wintertime.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It may have been, like, around-- I remember the snow because the Germans set up a camp just outside the village.
And we would go there.
And they had the POWs, the German prisoners, the ones that they had caught before they could leave into-- you know, retreat into Germany.
And I remember the snow.
Because I remember prisoners being marched naked in the snow by the Americans.
Yes.
--that the Americans had caught.
Yes.
And I remember the snow.
So it was around-- had to have been-- it was definitely wintertime.
Within the camp.
Oh, yeah.
I was on the outside of the barbed wires.
But I saw them.
I was very happy.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess for several months because then I was taken from the Cailacs.
No, probably winter of-- the-- it's very hard [SIGHS] to remember.
Could be '44.
No, it had to have been '45.
It had to have been the winter of '45, yes.
Yeah.
My next recollection, I guess we went into [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] summer and being told that we have new visitors by Mr. and Mrs.
Cailac.
They told me we were going to have company.
And--
I didn't know.
I didn't know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't know it had anything to do with me.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three people came, a woman-- a young woman-- and a man and a woman, older.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I vaguely remembered the man and the woman-- turned out that was my uncle, my Uncle Gabriel and my Aunt Paula, his wife.
And the young lady was my cousin, Stella, from London.
She is my father's brother's daughter.
And because I gave my name, they were able to trace me.
Her brother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was in the British army.
And I'm not sure, there were already organization-- Jewish organizations already being formed to try and trace all the survivors.
And they were able to locate me.
So she was the first civilian to leave England on a boat.
And she made her way to find me.
And my aunt and uncle were in Italy.
See, they made it out of occupied France.
And the Germans saved them.
Well, actually, the-- I shouldn't say the Germans.
The Italians gave them false identity.
And they survived in Italy.
And then when they came back to France, they, too, were looking for me and looking for my parents as well.
Anyway, they all made it back.
And I remember they were talking Yiddish.
And I said, that sounds very familiar.
Although I could not understand, I said, you know, it's, it's mamaloshen.
No, never knew her.
I didn't know her.
And--
A slight, yes, recollection.
It must have been many years that I hadn't seen him.
And then there were obviously discussions, not that I can recall of-- and then they left.
And when they left, I remember discussions between the Cailacs and themselves.
And then I was told some time later that they were taking me to a place where I would be with children and stuff.
It turns out that I didn't want to leave there-- them.
The Cailacs.
I was still waiting for my parents.
And--
Yeah.
Yeah.
They didn't know.
And legally, they couldn't take me because my parents had left them-- had left me with the Cailacs.
So it was decided that I was be-- put in an orphanage.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So legally, I had to go in an orphanage.
By that time, '45, maybe '46-- I was probably going on 9, 10.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they had to leave me there.
I think I spent there maybe a year or two.
Yes, uh-huh.
The orphanage was not too far from Paris.
It's called Andresy.
And it was an old castle that they had converted.
There were many children there.
[SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not really.
I do not remember traveling there.
I remember being left there.
The Cailacs told me.
And they took me there.
I don't recall getting there.
I think it was my first exposure to being with many children, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] not happy, didn't know where I was.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember my aunt and uncle coming to visit.
And I remember people [CRYING] knowing me.
But I didn't know them.
I don't know where they were from.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think these people had been in the labor camp with my parents and had survived.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they told me at that time that when the Germans were retreating and when they thought, you know, was going to be near the end, the French forces helped a lot of the Jews in the labor camp and took them into hiding in the mountains.
My father locked the house and stayed in the house-- locked the door.
So what do you think the Germans did?
They took them and, you know, deported them.
Who told you this?
The people who came [CRYING] to the orphanage.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they recounted the whole-- what happened.
They were the only ones that had been killed in the whole village-- my parents were.
The rest of the village survived.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
My father made many bad moves, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah.
So he chose not to go with the--
Right.
--the French forces.
Mm-hmm.
Don't ask me why, but they related that to me.
And they were the only ones--
That were taken from that village.
The whole village survived.
Don't forget, it was already 1944.
They survived through practically the whole war, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah.
Yes.
I remember that.
I cried-- [SOBBING] the anger, [SOBBING] stupidity.
But people make decisions.
They think it's for the best, [CRYING] yeah.
[CRYING] So I was alone.
[CRYING] And during my stay in the orphanage, we had a trip to England.
I recall that.
And because I had my cousins and my uncle still living-- that's the father of the cousin that had to come-- to see me in the village.
Yes, my Uncle Leon.
I was allowed to stay with them, and I met them.
So I think I stayed a couple weeks in London.
And--
Great-- [SOBBING] having family.
[SOBBING] My cousin at the time was not married.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] soon after, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was taken to my aunt and uncle in Paris.
That's my Uncle Gabriel.
Yes.
I went back to the orphanage.
And then I remember being put on a train.
I don't recall how I got there.
Maybe they came and get-- to get me.
And I stayed some time.
[SIGHS] I'm past 10 by then.
And I was taken to my aunt and uncle and was told I was going to live with them.
I think, at that point, I wanted to go to England.

[LAUGHS] But I found out later that there was, like-- a family council was formed of the uncle in England and the uncle in-- Gabriel in Paris-- he had no children-- and remaining cousins and aunts that were-- that had survived the war.
And it was decided that they would be my guardians.
There was a whole formality because I was, really, a ward of the state.
So the state declared my aunt and uncle my guardians.
And I was to live with them.
So I was taken out of the orphanage and brought to them to live in Paris.
And at the time, it was my-- bless you--
--it was my parent's apartment because my aunt and uncle had everything taken from them.
Their lives were spared, but nothing left.
The Germans had-- he, he was a bookbinder.
And they had taken all his equipment and ransacked the apartment.
It was no longer free.
So, for awhile, we lived in my, my own apartment-- in my parent's apartment.
And then we ultimately moved into another place.
[SIGHS] It was mixed emotions.
[CRYING]
Yes, it was.
Yes, it was.
We didn't stay long.
We didn't stay very long there.
But--
No.
No.
Well, basically, after all the moving around, I really wanted to go back to the Cailacs.
No, I wasn't allowed because at that point, I think they had been fighting to keep me.
And at that point, I wanted to go back with them.
So I was having a lot of nervous tantrums.
I remember throwing a lot of tantrums.
Yes, yes.
No, no.
Actually carefree.
They were very kind.
They were very loving.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a sense, I felt, even though we were the occupied France and the Germans were around, I felt somewhat protected by them.
I felt more fear with my parents, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just probably their own fear being reflected on me.
And--
Somewhat.
Yes, yes.
Yes, oh yes.
I remember Evelyn took me-- the Cailacs' daughter, Evelyn Cailac.
She took me.
And she said, come, we'll go see.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was-- I, I felt very happy because at that point, I had, I guess, feelings that something had happened to my parents.
But--
Oh yes.
Oh yes.
Absolutely, yeah.
Don't forget, you're, you're-- these were people who really had made a very serious effort to fight the war in their own way.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So yes, there was definitely elation.
And I felt my own, too.
I remember that, yeah.
